title: Severe Diabetic Ketoacidosis as Initial Presentation of New-Onset Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus in 8-Year-Old Girl with Glucose 580, pH 7.05, HCO3 8, Anion Gap 30, Kussmaul Respirations, and Altered Mental Status: Pediatric-Specific DKA Management with Cerebral Edema Prevention, Slow 48-Hour Fluid Replacement, Insulin Infusion 0.05-0.1 Units/kg/hr (No Bolus), Avoidance of Bicarbonate, Family-Centered Diabetes Education, and Multidisciplinary Endocrine + School + Community Coordination
subtitle: PEDIATRIC DKA (diabetic ketoacidosis) commonly presents as INITIAL manifestation of NEW-ONSET TYPE 1 DIABETES (~25-40% of new-onset T1DM presents in DKA — especially younger children + delayed diagnosis); CLASSIC TRIAD = HYPERGLYCEMIA + ANION-GAP METABOLIC ACIDOSIS + KETOSIS (urine + serum); CLINICAL FEATURES = polyuria + polydipsia + weight loss + fatigue + abdominal pain + nausea/vomiting (often misdiagnosed as gastroenteritis) + Kussmaul respirations (deep + rapid compensatory) + fruity/acetone breath + altered mental status + dehydration + tachycardia + hypotension late + signs of shock; DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA per ISPAD + ADA in pediatrics: BG >200 mg/dL + venous pH <7.3 OR bicarbonate <15 mEq/L + ketonemia/ketonuria; SEVERITY: MILD pH 7.2-7.3 + bicarb 10-15; MODERATE pH 7.1-<7.2 + bicarb 5-10; SEVERE pH <7.1 + bicarb <5 (THIS PATIENT severe — pH 7.05 + HCO3 8 + AG 30); CEREBRAL EDEMA = CATASTROPHIC COMPLICATION UNIQUE TO PEDIATRIC DKA — incidence 0.5-1% but causes 20-25% MORTALITY + 25% permanent neurologic disability in survivors; PATHOPHYSIOLOGY incompletely understood — likely combination of rapid osmotic shifts + cerebral hypoperfusion + ischemia-reperfusion + cytokine-mediated; RISK FACTORS = AGE <5 (highest), new-onset (vs known T1DM), severe acidosis at presentation (pH <7.1), severe dehydration, low PaCO2 (severe hyperventilation), low corrected sodium at presentation, treatment with bicarbonate (CONTRAINDICATED), rapid fluid administration, rapid drop in glucose + osmolality; CEREBRAL EDEMA WARNING SIGNS — onset typically 4-12 hr after initiation of treatment; HEADACHE + change in mental status (lethargy + irritability + agitation + confusion) + DECREASED HR + INCREASED BP (Cushing triad — bradycardia + HTN + irregular respirations) + abnormal respirations + papilledema + neurologic deficits + seizure + posturing; PEDIATRIC DKA management DIFFERENCES from adult: (1) SLOW FLUID REPLACEMENT over 48 hr (vs 24 hr in adult) using maintenance + deficit replacement; (2) NO INSULIN BOLUS (avoid rapid osmotic shifts; bolus may increase cerebral edema risk); (3) INSULIN INFUSION 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr (LOWER end recommended for younger + smaller children); some recent evidence supports 0.05 units/kg/hr equally effective + lower risk; (4) AVOID BICARBONATE (associated with cerebral edema + paradoxical CNS acidosis + minimal benefit unless arrhythmia-threatening hyperkalemia); (5) ADD D5 to IV fluids when glucose ≤300 mg/dL (vs ≤250 in adult — earlier addition to prevent rapid drop); (6) POTASSIUM replacement when K+ <5.0-5.5 (insulin shifts K+ intracellular; will drop with treatment); (7) FLUIDS = NS for first 1-2 hr to restore perfusion + then 0.45% NS or 0.9% NS at calculated rate (maintenance + deficit over 48 hr); (8) MONITOR Q1H — vital signs + neurochecks + glucose + ABG + electrolytes; (9) CEREBRAL EDEMA TREATMENT (when suspected) = MANNITOL 0.25-1 g/kg IV OR HYPERTONIC SALINE 3% 5-10 mL/kg + HOB 30° + REDUCE IV FLUID RATE + intubation if severe + ICU; (10) PSYCHOLOGICAL + family-centered care + age-appropriate engagement; FAMILY-CENTERED + LIFE-LONG diabetes education + insulin therapy education + CGM + pump + carbohydrate counting + age-appropriate self-care + school nurse coordination + camp/activities + sick day rules + hypoglycemia management + glucagon + emergency contacts + diabetes camp + support groups + community resources + insurance + technology

labs.n: PRESENTATION + new-onset T1DM diagnosis + DKA diagnosis
labs.v: 8 y/o female L. Park (3rd grader) presenting with severe DKA as INITIAL MANIFESTATION of new-onset Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus. PMH: previously healthy; usual immunizations up to date; school-age developmental milestones met; no chronic conditions; no medications. SOCIAL: lives with mother (38, accountant), father (41, software engineer), 5-yr-old brother; supportive nuclear family; no extended family chronic medical conditions concerning; insurance through father's employer (good coverage including pediatric endocrinology + diabetes supplies + CGM/pump); attends elementary school (3rd grade — does well academically); active in soccer + dance + reads + drawing. SYMPTOMS over past 3-4 weeks (per parental history reconstruction): (1) POLYURIA × 2 wk — waking 4-5 times/night to urinate (very unusual for her); enuresis recently; (2) POLYDIPSIA × 2 wk — drinking large amounts of water + asking for more frequently; (3) WEIGHT LOSS — 2.5 kg (5.5 lbs) over past month per pediatric chart (baseline 26.5 kg → current 24 kg = 9.4% body weight loss in 1 month — significant); (4) FATIGUE × 1 wk; (5) DECREASED APPETITE × few days; (6) ABDOMINAL PAIN × 1 day (diffuse, mild); (7) NAUSEA + VOMITING × 3 episodes today; (8) LETHARGY + difficulty arousing this morning. PARENTS ATTRIBUTED early symptoms to growth spurt + recent stress (school + brother + family activities) + flu season; brought to ED today when lethargy was severe + unusual. ED ARRIVAL 0700 — child lethargic + confused + dehydrated + tachycardic + tachypneic (Kussmaul) + FRUITY ACETONE BREATH + abdominal pain + altered mental status. RAPID assessment + IV access × 2 + labs sent + pediatric ED activated + ICU consultation. ED LABS: GLUCOSE 580 mg/dL (severe hyperglycemia; normal <100 fasting; diabetes diagnostic ≥126 fasting or ≥200 random with symptoms); pH 7.05 (severe metabolic acidosis); PCO2 12 (low — Kussmaul respiratory compensation); HCO3 8 (severe metabolic acidosis); ANION GAP 28 (normal 8-12 — elevated AG indicates AG metabolic acidosis from ketoacids); SERUM KETONES POSITIVE + URINE KETONES LARGE (3+); BMP: Na 138 (CORRECTED 145.7 with adjustment for hyperglycemia using formula corrected Na = measured Na + 1.6 × (BG-100)/100); K+ 4.2 (within normal but pseudo-normal due to acidosis-induced extracellular shift + will fall rapidly with insulin); Cl 102; BUN 28 (elevated — dehydration); Cr 0.8 (normal for age); calcium 9.6; magnesium 1.9; phosphate 4.5; CBC: Hgb 13.8 (hemoconcentration from dehydration); WBC 18,000 (mild leukocytosis — stress response common in DKA, not necessarily infection; cultures sent to rule out); platelets 380; LFT normal; lipase normal (abdominal pain in DKA common — not pancreatitis); HbA1c 13.2% (markedly elevated reflecting average glucose ~340 over past 2-3 months — indicating LONG-STANDING undiagnosed hyperglycemia); calculated osmolality 318 mOsm/kg (markedly elevated); type 1 diabetes autoantibodies sent (GAD + IA-2 + ZnT8 + insulin autoantibodies — will confirm autoimmune T1DM vs T2DM in this age + presentation typical T1DM). DIAGNOSIS = T1DM new-onset presenting in severe DKA + dehydration estimated 7-10% (clinical signs + lab data) + altered mental status from severe metabolic acidosis + hyperosmolality. CEREBRAL EDEMA RISK ASSESSMENT — age 8 (moderate risk — younger than 5 highest), new-onset (HIGH risk vs known T1DM), severe acidosis pH 7.05 (HIGH risk vs >7.1), severe dehydration, severe hyperosmolality, treatment with bicarbonate CONTRAINDICATED — careful management essential.
labs.r: PEDIATRIC DKA as initial T1DM presentation common (~25-40% of new-onset T1DM presents in DKA); RECOGNITION + diagnosis + management foundational; CEREBRAL EDEMA RISK assessment essential. RN role: rapid recognition + IV access + labs + ICU activation + family communication + initial stabilization.

labs.n: PEDIATRIC DKA management — KEY differences from adult
labs.v: PEDIATRIC DKA management per ISPAD (International Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Diabetes) + ADA guidelines with KEY DIFFERENCES from adult DKA: (1) SLOW FLUID REPLACEMENT over 48 HOURS (NOT 24 hr like adult) — prevents rapid osmotic shifts + cerebral edema; CALCULATE = MAINTENANCE (Holliday-Segar: 100 mL/kg first 10 kg + 50 mL/kg next 10 kg + 20 mL/kg/day for >20 kg) + DEFICIT (estimated %dehydration × weight in kg × 10 = mL; typically 5-10%); split over 48 hr; e.g., 24 kg child with 10% dehydration = maintenance 1580 mL/day × 2 days + deficit 2400 mL / 48 hr = ~50 mL/hr maintenance + ~50 mL/hr deficit = ~100 mL/hr total; (2) INITIAL FLUID = 0.9% NS bolus 10-20 mL/kg over 30-60 min for hemodynamic instability (single bolus typically sufficient; avoid multiple boluses as they may increase cerebral edema risk); transition to 0.9% NS or 0.45% NS at calculated rate; (3) NO INSULIN BOLUS — rapid osmotic shifts increase cerebral edema risk; (4) INSULIN INFUSION 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr (NOT loading bolus; can start lower 0.05 in younger children + recent evidence supports equivalent efficacy with lower risk; titrate to glucose drop 50-100 mg/dL/hr — TOO RAPID drop increases cerebral edema risk; TOO SLOW prolongs treatment); (5) ADD D5 to IV FLUIDS when glucose ≤300 mg/dL (NOT 250 like adults — earlier addition prevents rapid drop in osmolality + cerebral edema risk); D5 can be added to 0.45% NS or 0.9% NS depending on sodium status; (6) AVOID BICARBONATE (CONTRAINDICATED per ISPAD — associated with cerebral edema + paradoxical CNS acidosis when CO2 crosses BBB faster than HCO3 + worsens K+ shift + minimal benefit; consider ONLY if life-threatening arrhythmia from hyperkalemia or severe acidosis pH <6.9 with hemodynamic compromise unresponsive to fluid + insulin — give VERY cautiously); (7) POTASSIUM replacement — add K+ to IV fluids when K+ <5.0-5.5 + UOP adequate; insulin shifts K+ intracellular + K+ falls rapidly; typically add 20-40 mEq/L KCl + monitor K+ q2h; if K+ <3.5 + insulin already running → REDUCE insulin rate temporarily + replace K+ aggressively + ECG monitoring; (8) PHOSPHATE replacement controversial — some protocols replace if PO4 <1.0 (severe); routine replacement not recommended (does not improve outcomes + may worsen hypocalcemia); (9) AVOID NPO entirely — child can have small amounts of clear fluids when tolerating after initial stabilization (helps hydration + comfort); (10) CONTINUOUS MONITORING q1h vital signs + neurochecks + glucose + I/O; q2-4h electrolytes + ABG; cerebral edema surveillance throughout; (11) RESOLUTION of DKA = pH >7.3 + bicarb >18 + AG closed (≤12) + glucose <250 + tolerating PO; transition to SQ insulin (basal-bolus regimen) when AG closed + tolerating PO; OVERLAP insulin infusion with first SQ dose (continue drip 30-60 min after first SQ rapid-acting injection to bridge); discontinue drip once SQ insulin onset achieved.
labs.r: PEDIATRIC DKA management DIFFERS from adult — slower fluid replacement (48 hr) + no insulin bolus + lower insulin infusion rate + earlier D5 addition + no bicarbonate + careful K+ management + cerebral edema prevention. Foundation of pediatric DKA care. RN role: protocol adherence + monitoring + escalation.

labs.n: CEREBRAL EDEMA in pediatric DKA — recognition + prevention + treatment
labs.v: CEREBRAL EDEMA = CATASTROPHIC complication unique to PEDIATRIC DKA (rare in adult DKA); INCIDENCE 0.5-1% of pediatric DKA but causes ~20-25% MORTALITY + ~25% PERMANENT NEUROLOGIC DISABILITY in survivors (cognitive impairment + motor + sensory + behavioral); ONSET typically 4-12 HOURS after initiation of DKA treatment (rarely up to 48 hr); PATHOPHYSIOLOGY incompletely understood — proposed mechanisms include rapid osmotic shifts (cellular swelling with rapid drop in extracellular osmolality) + cerebral hypoperfusion + ischemia-reperfusion injury + cytokine-mediated inflammation + vasogenic edema + cellular ionic dysregulation. RISK FACTORS: (1) AGE <5 (HIGHEST risk); (2) NEW-ONSET diabetes (vs known T1DM); (3) SEVERE ACIDOSIS at presentation (pH <7.1); (4) SEVERE DEHYDRATION; (5) LOW PCO2 (severe hyperventilation indicating severe acidosis); (6) LOW CORRECTED SODIUM at presentation or failure of Na to rise during treatment; (7) HIGHER BUN at presentation; (8) TREATMENT WITH BICARBONATE (CONTRAINDICATED); (9) RAPID administration of fluid (avoid excessive volume boluses + use slow calculated rate); (10) RAPID drop in glucose + osmolality (too fast insulin or excessive fluid); (11) FAILURE of Na to rise as glucose falls during treatment (suggests inadequate sodium replacement). WARNING SIGNS — onset typically 4-12 hr after treatment initiation: (1) HEADACHE (any new headache concerning); (2) Change in MENTAL STATUS — lethargy + irritability + agitation + confusion + delayed response + unusual behavior; (3) DECREASED HR + INCREASED BP (Cushing triad — bradycardia + hypertension + irregular respirations from increased ICP); (4) PAPILLEDEMA (later sign); (5) DECREASED level of consciousness (drowsy → obtunded → coma); (6) PUPILLARY CHANGES (sluggish + dilated + asymmetric); (7) ABNORMAL RESPIRATIONS (irregular + apnea); (8) POSTURING (decerebrate or decorticate); (9) SEIZURE; (10) FOCAL neurologic deficits; (11) URINARY/STOOL INCONTINENCE atypical for child. SCREENING using clinical neuro-criteria (Muir + Glaser modified) — ANY ONE major criterion (abnormal motor or verbal response to pain + abnormal posturing + cranial nerve palsy + abnormal respiratory pattern) OR TWO minor criteria (altered mental status + sustained heart rate decrease + age-inappropriate incontinence) — TREAT IMMEDIATELY. TREATMENT when cerebral edema suspected: (1) IMMEDIATE recognition + ICU intensivist activation; (2) MANNITOL 0.25-1 g/kg IV over 20 min (typical 1 g/kg if severe) — reduces ICP via osmotic effect; (3) ALTERNATIVE HYPERTONIC SALINE 3% 5-10 mL/kg IV over 30 min — comparable efficacy + may be preferred for hyponatremia; (4) HEAD OF BED 30° elevation + avoid neck flexion + minimize stimulation; (5) REDUCE IV FLUID RATE — slow + reassess; (6) MAINTAIN normocapnia (PaCO2 35-45); avoid hyperventilation as long-term therapy (transient hyperventilation PaCO2 30-35 for acute herniation only); (7) MAINTAIN MAP for cerebral perfusion pressure — vasopressors if needed; (8) INTUBATE for airway protection if severe altered mental status (GCS ≤8) + cerebral edema management; (9) NEUROIMAGING — emergent CT head (will show cerebral edema; rule out other causes); (10) ICU admission + ICP monitoring consideration; (11) DEXAMETHASONE has been used but not standard recommendation; (12) AVOID hypotonic fluid + aggressive osmotic shifts; (13) PROGNOSIS variable + early recognition + treatment improves outcomes. PREVENTION through CAREFUL DKA MANAGEMENT: (1) SLOW fluid replacement over 48 hr (NOT 24 hr); (2) NO INSULIN BOLUS; (3) INSULIN 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr (lower in younger children); (4) AVOID BICARBONATE; (5) ADD D5 to fluids when glucose ≤300; (6) AVOID rapid drop in glucose (target 50-100 mg/dL/hr); (7) MONITOR neurochecks q1h with intensive surveillance × 24 hr; (8) AVOID excessive volume; (9) MAINTAIN sodium rise as glucose falls (corrected Na should remain stable or rise during treatment — if falling = warning sign); (10) MULTIDISCIPLINARY team + pediatric intensivist + endocrinology.
labs.r: CEREBRAL EDEMA = CATASTROPHIC complication unique to pediatric DKA — recognition + prevention + treatment. Warning signs at 4-12 hr post-treatment initiation. Cushing triad + altered mental status + abnormal respirations + neuro deficits. TREATMENT = MANNITOL 0.25-1 g/kg OR hypertonic saline 3% 5-10 mL/kg + HOB 30° + reduce IV fluid rate + ICU + intubation if severe. RN role: neurochecks q1h + recognize early + escalate + administer treatment.

labs.v: INSULIN THERAPY TRANSITION from IV drip to SQ basal-bolus in pediatric DKA: TIMING = when DKA resolved (pH >7.3 + bicarb >18 + AG closed ≤12 + glucose <250 + tolerating PO) + child stable + day shift for educational opportunity; OVERLAP IV insulin with FIRST SQ dose — continue IV drip 30-60 min after first SQ rapid-acting injection to bridge gap (SQ onset ~15 min) + then discontinue IV; SUBSEQUENT timing per regimen. BASAL-BOLUS REGIMEN for new-onset T1DM in pediatric DKA: (1) BASAL (long-acting): GLARGINE 0.4 units/kg/day (this patient 24 kg × 0.4 = 9.6 units; round to 10 units) given QHS or AM; alternative INSULIN DETEMIR (twice daily) or INSULIN DEGLUDEC (daily, longer-acting, less peaking — preferred for younger children with consistent eating); covers basal metabolic insulin needs; (2) BOLUS (rapid-acting): INSULIN LISPRO or ASPART or GLULISINE 0.1 units/kg/meal as initial estimate; carb counting 1:15 ratio (1 unit insulin per 15 g carbs — typical starting; will adjust with experience); correction factor (sensitivity): 1500 rule (1500/total daily dose = points BG drops per unit insulin); e.g., TDD 24 units/day → 1500/24 = 62.5 mg/dL drop per unit; correction = (current BG - target BG)/correction factor; usually given before meals + at bedtime if elevated; (3) TOTAL DAILY DOSE typically 0.5-1.0 units/kg/day (children) — starts lower + titrates; honeymoon phase common in new-onset where endogenous insulin partially returns (months to years) + needs may decrease initially. CONTINUOUS GLUCOSE MONITORING (CGM) — strongly recommended for pediatric T1DM + ADA + ISPAD; reduces HbA1c + hypoglycemia + improves quality of life; Dexcom G7 + Libre 3 + Medtronic Guardian commonly used in pediatric; insurance coverage variable. INSULIN PUMP — option for some patients; reduces hypoglycemia + flexibility; requires substantial education + maturity; for older children + capable + interested. SICK DAY RULES = increased monitoring + glucose checks q1-2h + ketone checks + extra fluids + don't stop insulin (need MORE insulin during illness) + correction doses + early healthcare contact + emergency plan. HYPOGLYCEMIA management = signs (sweating + tremor + tachycardia + weakness + confusion + altered mental status + seizure + coma) + treatment (15-15 rule = 15 g rapid carbs + recheck 15 min + repeat if still low; juice + glucose tablets + gel); GLUCAGON 0.5-1 mg IM/SQ/IN (intranasal Baqsimi 3 mg available) for severe hypoglycemia with altered mental status + unable to eat. EDUCATION components: T1DM overview + insulin mechanism + injection technique + sites + rotation + CGM/pump + carb counting + meal planning + hypoglycemia recognition + treatment + glucagon training + sick day rules + exercise + alcohol (older children) + school nurse coordination + camp + travel + emergency contacts + community resources + insurance + emotional + family support. SCHOOL COORDINATION — 504 plan + school nurse involvement + diabetes management plan + emergency contacts + supplies + teachers + classmates + privacy + advocacy.
labs.r: INSULIN therapy transition + basal-bolus regimen + CGM + sick day rules + hypoglycemia management + diabetes education + school coordination = comprehensive T1DM management. RN role: education + administration + monitoring + family + multidisciplinary coordination.

labs.v: FAMILY-CENTERED + AGE-APPROPRIATE CARE for 8-year-old with new-onset T1DM in severe DKA: (1) PARENTS as primary decision-makers + supportive presence + rooming-in encouraged; engage as partners; honest + clear + compassionate communication; (2) CHILD development — 8 yr old is school-age (concrete operational per Piaget; able to understand cause-effect + basic explanations + medical concepts with age-appropriate language); engage child in age-appropriate decisions + explanations + procedures; preserve dignity + privacy + age-appropriate engagement; (3) CHILD LIFE SPECIALIST consultation for procedures + emotional support + age-appropriate explanations + therapeutic play + coping strategies + sibling support; medical play + dolls + books + apps + games; (4) PROCEDURAL PREPARATION + comfort positioning + parental presence + distraction + numbing cream for IV starts + age-appropriate explanations; (5) FAMILY MEETING + diagnosis + treatment + future + lifestyle changes + insurance + support + emotional impact on whole family; (6) SIBLING SUPPORT — 5-yr-old brother visit + age-appropriate explanation about sister's illness + reassurance + maintain his routines + grandparents involvement; family equilibrium important; (7) PEDIATRIC ENDOCRINOLOGY + DIABETES EDUCATOR comprehensive education in pieces (overwhelming if all at once) + tailored to family literacy + cultural sensitivity; (8) PSYCHOSOCIAL support + anticipatory grief over lost 'normal' + chronic illness adjustment + identity + future; mental health screening + referral if needed; long-term may include child + family therapy; (9) SCHOOL coordination + 504 plan + diabetes management plan + emergency supplies + privacy; school nurse + teacher + classmates education + advocacy; (10) COMMUNITY resources + diabetes camps (e.g., American Diabetes Association camps) + support groups + online communities + advocacy organizations (JDRF, ADA) + family network; (11) INSURANCE + financial — diabetes supplies expensive; insurance benefits + Medicaid eligibility + pharmaceutical assistance + community resources; (12) CULTURAL + SPIRITUAL — family identifies as Korean American + grandparents in Korea + chaplain support if requested + traditional foods + cultural considerations + interpreter services if needed; family is English-fluent + bilingual home; (13) CHAPLAIN visit offered + family receptive — supportive presence + meaning-making + family rituals respected; (14) DISCHARGE planning + follow-up + outpatient pediatric endocrinology within 1-2 weeks + ongoing education + CGM/pump setup + supplies + emergency plan + school coordination + community resources. nursesNote: 0700 — 8 y/o F L. Park presented to ED via parents who brought her in for 3-day worsening symptoms + lethargy this morning + difficulty arousing. HISTORY per parents: previously healthy 3rd grader; 3-4 weeks of POLYURIA (waking 4-5x/night to urinate × 2 wk + enuresis recently) + POLYDIPSIA (drinking large amounts × 2 wk + asking frequently) + WEIGHT LOSS (2.5 kg over past month per pediatric chart — 9.4% body weight loss) + FATIGUE × 1 wk + DECREASED APPETITE × few days + 1 DAY abdominal pain + N/V × 3 episodes + this morning LETHARGY + difficulty arousing. Parents attributed early symptoms to growth + stress + flu; brought to ED when lethargy severe + unusual. LABS confirm SEVERE DKA with new-onset T1DM: GLUCOSE 580 mg/dL (severe hyperglycemia) + pH 7.05 (SEVERE metabolic acidosis) + PCO2 12 (low — respiratory compensation Kussmaul) + HCO3 8 (severe metabolic acidosis) + AG 28 (markedly elevated; normal 8-12) + SERUM + URINE KETONES POSITIVE (3+) + Na 138 (CORRECTED 145.7 for hyperglycemia) + K+ 4.2 (within normal — pseudo-normal due to acidosis; will fall rapidly with insulin) + Cl 102 + BUN 28 (elevated — dehydration) + Cr 0.8 (normal for age) + Ca 9.6 + Mg 1.9 + Phos 4.5 + Hgb 13.8 (hemoconcentration from dehydration) + WBC 18,000 (mild leukocytosis stress response of DKA not necessarily infection; cultures sent) + platelets 380 + LFT normal + lipase normal (abdominal pain in DKA common — not pancreatitis) + HbA1c 13.2% (markedly elevated reflecting long-standing hyperglycemia ~340 average past 2-3 mo) + calculated osmolality 318 mOsm/kg (severely elevated; normal 285-295) + T1DM autoantibodies sent (GAD + IA-2 + ZnT8 + insulin autoantibodies — will confirm autoimmune T1DM). DEHYDRATION estimated 7-10% based on clinical signs + lab data; SEVERITY = severe DKA (pH <7.1 + HCO3 <5); CEREBRAL EDEMA RISK ASSESSMENT — age 8 (moderate; younger than 5 highest), NEW-ONSET (high risk), severe acidosis pH 7.05 (high), severe dehydration, severe hyperosmolality — careful management essential to prevent cerebral edema. providerOrders: ADMIT to PICU Bed 3 (single-room continuous monitoring + family-centered environment)

DIAGNOSIS: severe diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) as initial presentation of new-onset Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus in 8-year-old female; pH 7.05 + HCO3 8 + AG 28 + glucose 580 + ketones positive + 9.4% body weight loss + 7-10% dehydration
CODE STATUS: FULL CODE; parents primary decision-makers; advance directives not applicable
PEDIATRIC DKA MANAGEMENT per ISPAD + ADA protocol:
FLUID REPLACEMENT over 48 HOURS (NOT 24 hr like adult — prevents cerebral edema)
Initial 0.9% NS bolus 20 mL/kg = 480 mL given SLOWLY over 1 hr (single bolus typically sufficient; avoid multiple boluses)
Then 0.45% NS at maintenance + deficit over 48 hr = ~100 mL/hr total (maintenance 1580 mL/day Holliday-Segar + deficit 2400 mL/48 hr)
ADD D5 to IV fluids when glucose ≤300 mg/dL (EARLIER than adult 250 — prevents rapid drop in osmolality + cerebral edema risk)
Continue 0.45% NS + D5 until DKA resolved + tolerating PO + SQ insulin
NO INSULIN BOLUS (avoid rapid osmotic shifts + cerebral edema risk)
INSULIN INFUSION 0.1 units/kg/hr = 2.4 units/hr via dedicated IV line (consider 0.05 units/kg/hr in younger children + with recent evidence)
Target glucose decrease 50-100 mg/dL/hr (NOT faster — cerebral edema risk)

AVOID BICARBONATE (CONTRAINDICATED in pediatric DKA per ISPAD — associated with cerebral edema + paradoxical CNS acidosis + minimal benefit; reserved for life-threatening arrhythmia or pH <6.9 with hemodynamic compromise)
POTASSIUM: not initially (K+ 4.2 normal); ADD KCl 20-40 mEq/L to IV fluids when K+ <5.0-5.5 + UOP adequate; recheck q2h; if K+ <3.5 REDUCE insulin rate temporarily + replace K+ + ECG
PHOSPHATE replacement only if severe <1.0 (controversial routine replacement not recommended)
MONITORING (PICU-level):
Continuous cardiac monitoring + arterial line + central line + Foley + IV access × 2
Q1H vital signs + NEURO CHECKS (cerebral edema surveillance) + glucose + I/O
Q2-4H electrolytes (BMP) + ABG + ketones
HOURLY UOP + I/O strict

NEUROCHECKS for CEREBRAL EDEMA — headache + change in mental status (lethargy + irritability + agitation + confusion) + Cushing triad (bradycardia + HTN + irregular respirations) + papilledema + neurologic deficits + abnormal respirations + posturing + seizure; ANY warning sign = IMMEDIATE escalation + treatment
Cerebral edema bedside treatment available: MANNITOL 0.25-1 g/kg IV (24 kg × 0.25-1 = 6-24 mL of 20% mannitol 0.25-1 g/mL) OR HYPERTONIC SALINE 3% 5-10 mL/kg IV (24 kg × 5-10 = 120-240 mL)
Tissue perfusion + mental status + cap refill + skin
Family at bedside + emotional support
TRANSITION TO SQ INSULIN (when DKA resolved):
Criteria: pH >7.3 + bicarb >18 + AG closed ≤12 + glucose <250 + tolerating PO + child stable + day shift
OVERLAP IV with first SQ dose — continue IV drip 30-60 min after first SQ rapid-acting injection
BASAL-BOLUS regimen for new-onset T1DM:
BASAL: INSULIN GLARGINE 0.4 units/kg/day = 9.6 → 10 units SQ QHS (or AM)
BOLUS: INSULIN LISPRO or ASPART 0.1 units/kg/meal as initial + correction; carb counting 1:15 ratio + correction factor (1500 rule)
TDD typically 0.5-1.0 units/kg/day initially
Honeymoon phase common in new-onset — needs may decrease initially over months-years

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this severe pediatric DKA presentation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically + pediatric-specific significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: SEVERE PEDIATRIC DKA = HYPERGLYCEMIA (glucose 580) + ANION-GAP METABOLIC ACIDOSIS (pH 7.05 + HCO3 8 + AG 28) + KETOSIS (serum + urine 3+); presenting as INITIAL MANIFESTATION of NEW-ONSET T1DM (HbA1c 13.2% reflecting 2-3 mo long-standing hyperglycemia; T1DM autoantibodies pending); CLINICAL TRIAD + classic features = polyuria + polydipsia + weight loss + Kussmaul respirations + fruity breath + altered mental status + dehydration
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severe DKA diagnosis + new-onset T1DM. Foundation for management. RECOGNITION + diagnosis essential. RN role: assessment + lab interpretation + escalation.

opts.text: CEREBRAL EDEMA RISK = catastrophic complication unique to pediatric DKA (~0.5-1% incidence but 20-25% mortality + 25% permanent neurologic disability); RISK FACTORS for this patient = AGE 8 (moderate; <5 highest risk), NEW-ONSET T1DM (high risk vs known), SEVERE ACIDOSIS pH 7.05 (high), severe dehydration + hyperosmolality; onset typically 4-12 hr AFTER initiating treatment; WARNING SIGNS = HEADACHE + change in mental status + Cushing triad (bradycardia + HTN + irregular respirations) + papilledema + neurologic deficits + abnormal respirations + posturing + seizure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — cerebral edema risk + recognition. Catastrophic complication. Prevention through careful management + surveillance. RN role: neurochecks q1h + recognize warning signs + immediate escalation.

opts.text: PEDIATRIC DKA MANAGEMENT differences from adult: (1) SLOW fluid replacement over 48 hr (NOT 24 hr); (2) NO INSULIN BOLUS (rapid shifts increase cerebral edema risk); (3) INSULIN 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr (lower in younger children); (4) AVOID BICARBONATE (CONTRAINDICATED per ISPAD); (5) ADD D5 to fluids when glucose ≤300 mg/dL (EARLIER than adult 250); (6) POTASSIUM when K+ <5.0-5.5 + UOP adequate; (7) maintenance + deficit fluid calculation over 48 hr
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pediatric DKA management differences. Foundation for safe care. Prevention of cerebral edema. RN role: protocol adherence + dose calculation + medication administration + monitoring.

opts.text: INITIAL FLUID + INSULIN PLAN: 0.9% NS bolus 20 mL/kg = 480 mL slowly over 1 hr (single bolus typically sufficient); transition to 0.45% NS at calculated maintenance + deficit rate ~100 mL/hr over 48 hr; ADD D5 when glucose ≤300; INSULIN INFUSION 0.1 units/kg/hr (no bolus); NO BICARBONATE; potassium replacement when needed; continuous monitoring
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — initial management plan + dose calculations. Foundation for treatment. Standard per ISPAD + ADA pediatric DKA protocols.

opts.text: CONTINUOUS MONITORING q1h vital signs + NEURO CHECKS + glucose + I/O + tissue perfusion + cardiac monitoring + arterial line + central line + Foley + IV access × 2; q2-4h electrolytes + ABG + ketones; UOP hourly + I/O strict; bedside mannitol + hypertonic saline available immediately for cerebral edema treatment if needed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — comprehensive PICU-level monitoring. Cerebral edema surveillance + DKA progression monitoring. Standard practice. RN role: continuous bedside monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: TRANSITION TO SQ INSULIN when DKA resolved (pH >7.3 + bicarb >18 + AG closed + glucose <250 + tolerating PO); BASAL-BOLUS regimen — GLARGINE 0.4 units/kg/day = 10 units QHS + LISPRO/ASPART 0.1 units/kg/meal + correction; CGM placement; insulin pump consideration; carb counting; sick day rules; hypoglycemia management; glucagon training
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — insulin therapy transition + long-term diabetes management. Standard practice. Foundation for life-long management.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY + FAMILY-CENTERED + AGE-APPROPRIATE care: pediatric endocrinologist + diabetes educator + child life specialist + social worker + chaplain + dietitian + pediatric intensivist + nurses + family + school + community; cultural sensitivity (Korean American + grandparents from Korea + chaplain); insurance + financial counseling; psychosocial screening + mental health if needed; long-term partnership
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multidisciplinary engagement + family-centered care + age-appropriate engagement. Standard pediatric care. RN role: coordination + advocacy + education + emotional support + cultural humility.

opts.text: DISCHARGE PLANNING + outpatient follow-up + diabetes camps + community resources + school 504 plan + insurance + technology (CGM/pump) + supplies + emergency plan + glucagon + ongoing education + long-term T1DM partnership; multidisciplinary outpatient care; mental health + family adjustment + community support
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — comprehensive discharge + long-term T1DM management. Foundation for safe transition + lifelong care.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. No medication contraindications. Not the priority cue but important for medication safety.

opts.text: Initiate SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg IV bolus immediately to rapidly correct the severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.05 + HCO3 8) — bicarbonate will rapidly normalize pH + improve clinical status
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. BICARBONATE IS CONTRAINDICATED in pediatric DKA per ISPAD — associated with CEREBRAL EDEMA + paradoxical CNS acidosis (CO2 crosses blood-brain barrier faster than HCO3 → worsens CNS acidosis) + worsens potassium shift + has MINIMAL CLINICAL BENEFIT. ACIDOSIS in DKA RESOLVES with appropriate fluid + insulin (ketoacid clearance). BICARBONATE reserved ONLY for life-threatening arrhythmia from hyperkalemia or severe acidosis pH <6.9 with hemodynamic compromise unresponsive to standard treatment. Standard practice: NO bicarbonate in routine pediatric DKA.

opts.text: Administer INSULIN BOLUS 0.1 units/kg IV push = 2.4 units to rapidly lower the severely elevated glucose 580 + then start insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr — bolus accelerates correction + reduces risk of complications
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. INSULIN BOLUS is CONTRAINDICATED in pediatric DKA — increases cerebral edema risk through rapid osmotic shifts. Standard practice: INSULIN INFUSION 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr WITHOUT bolus; target glucose decrease 50-100 mg/dL/hr (NOT faster — too rapid drop increases cerebral edema risk).

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What pediatric DKA + endocrine + safety principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: PEDIATRIC DKA = HYPERGLYCEMIA + ANION-GAP METABOLIC ACIDOSIS + KETOSIS; SEVERITY by pH + bicarb: MILD pH 7.2-7.3 + bicarb 10-15; MODERATE pH 7.1-<7.2 + bicarb 5-10; SEVERE pH <7.1 + bicarb <5; CLINICAL TRIAD + polyuria + polydipsia + weight loss + Kussmaul + fruity breath + altered mental status + dehydration; ~25-40% of new-onset T1DM presents in DKA
opts.rat: CORRECT — pediatric DKA framework + diagnostic criteria + severity + presentation. Standard knowledge. Foundation for recognition + management.

opts.text: CEREBRAL EDEMA = unique catastrophic complication of pediatric DKA (~0.5-1% incidence; 20-25% mortality + 25% permanent disability); pathophysiology = rapid osmotic shifts + cerebral hypoperfusion + ischemia-reperfusion + cytokine + cellular ionic dysregulation; RISK FACTORS = age <5 highest + new-onset + severe acidosis pH <7.1 + severe dehydration + low PCO2 + low corrected Na + higher BUN + bicarbonate (CONTRAINDICATED) + rapid fluid + rapid glucose drop + failure of Na to rise; WARNING SIGNS at 4-12 hr = headache + change in mental status + Cushing triad + neurologic deficits
opts.rat: CORRECT — cerebral edema framework + risk factors + warning signs. Standard pediatric DKA knowledge. RN role: recognize + prevent + treat.

opts.text: PEDIATRIC DKA management differences from adult: (1) SLOW fluid 48 hr (vs 24 hr); (2) NO INSULIN BOLUS; (3) INSULIN 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr; (4) AVOID BICARBONATE; (5) ADD D5 when glucose ≤300 (EARLIER than adult); (6) POTASSIUM when K+ <5.0-5.5; (7) maintenance + deficit calculation; (8) NEUROCHECKS q1h cerebral edema surveillance; (9) CEREBRAL EDEMA treatment = MANNITOL 0.25-1 g/kg OR hypertonic saline 3% 5-10 mL/kg + HOB 30° + reduce IV rate + ICU + intubation if severe
opts.rat: CORRECT — pediatric DKA management framework + differences from adult + cerebral edema treatment. Standard ISPAD + ADA pediatric protocols. RN role: protocol adherence + monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: BICARBONATE in pediatric DKA = CONTRAINDICATED per ISPAD — associated with CEREBRAL EDEMA + paradoxical CNS acidosis (CO2 crosses BBB faster than HCO3) + worsens K+ shift + minimal clinical benefit; ACIDOSIS RESOLVES with appropriate fluid + insulin (ketoacid clearance); BICARBONATE reserved ONLY for life-threatening arrhythmia from hyperkalemia or severe acidosis pH <6.9 with hemodynamic compromise unresponsive
opts.rat: CORRECT — bicarbonate contraindication + rare exception. Standard practice. RN role: recognize + advocate against bicarbonate use + escalate if provider proposes.

opts.text: INSULIN management in pediatric DKA: NO BOLUS (avoid rapid osmotic shifts + cerebral edema risk); INSULIN INFUSION 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr (lower in younger children + recent evidence supports 0.05 effective + lower risk); via DEDICATED IV line (no carrier fluid contamination); target glucose drop 50-100 mg/dL/hr (NOT faster — cerebral edema risk); ADD D5 when glucose ≤300; transition to SQ basal-bolus when DKA resolved + overlap 30-60 min after first SQ rapid-acting dose
opts.rat: CORRECT — insulin management framework. Standard practice. Foundation for safe DKA treatment + transition to long-term management.

opts.text: POTASSIUM management in pediatric DKA: K+ at presentation may appear normal (pseudo-normal due to acidosis-induced extracellular shift); INSULIN shifts K+ INTRACELLULAR + K+ DROPS RAPIDLY with treatment; ADD KCl 20-40 mEq/L to IV fluids when K+ <5.0-5.5 + UOP adequate (urine output >1 mL/kg/hr in pediatric); MONITOR K+ q2h; IF K+ <3.5 + insulin running = REDUCE insulin rate temporarily + replace K+ aggressively + ECG monitoring; CARDIAC monitoring throughout
opts.rat: CORRECT — potassium management framework. Standard practice. Foundation for cardiac safety during DKA treatment. RN role: q2h K+ monitoring + add KCl + escalate if low + cardiac monitoring.

opts.text: TRANSITION TO SQ INSULIN when DKA resolved (pH >7.3 + bicarb >18 + AG closed ≤12 + glucose <250 + tolerating PO) + child stable + day shift for education; OVERLAP IV with first SQ dose 30-60 min; BASAL-BOLUS regimen — GLARGINE 0.4 units/kg/day basal + LISPRO/ASPART 0.1 units/kg/meal + correction; CGM placement; insulin pump consideration; carb counting + sick day rules + hypoglycemia management + glucagon
opts.rat: CORRECT — insulin transition + long-term diabetes management. Standard practice. Foundation for lifelong T1DM management.

opts.text: Initiate SODIUM BICARBONATE 1 mEq/kg IV bolus immediately to rapidly correct severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.05) — bicarbonate will rapidly normalize pH + improve clinical status
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. BICARBONATE CONTRAINDICATED in pediatric DKA — associated with cerebral edema + paradoxical CNS acidosis + worsens K+ + minimal benefit. ACIDOSIS resolves with fluid + insulin. Reserved only for life-threatening situations.

opts.text: Administer INSULIN BOLUS 0.1 units/kg IV push = 2.4 units to rapidly lower the severely elevated glucose + then start infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. INSULIN BOLUS CONTRAINDICATED in pediatric DKA — increases cerebral edema risk through rapid osmotic shifts. Standard: INFUSION without bolus + target glucose drop 50-100 mg/dL/hr.

opts.text: RAPID FLUID REPLACEMENT over 12-24 hours to quickly correct the dehydration + hypotension; faster correction = faster clinical improvement + earlier discharge
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. RAPID FLUID REPLACEMENT increases cerebral edema risk in pediatric DKA. STANDARD = SLOW REPLACEMENT over 48 hours (NOT 24 hr like adult) to allow gradual osmotic shifts + prevent cerebral edema. Patient hemodynamics adequate after initial bolus + gradual replacement is safer + still effective.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses

opts.text: Administer INSULIN BOLUS 0.1 units/kg IV push = 2.4 units to rapidly lower the severely elevated glucose + then start infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. INSULIN BOLUS CONTRAINDICATED in pediatric DKA — increases CEREBRAL EDEMA risk through rapid osmotic shifts. Standard: INFUSION without bolus + target glucose drop 50-100 mg/dL/hr.

opts.text: RAPID FLUID REPLACEMENT over 12-24 hours to quickly correct the dehydration + hypotension; faster correction = faster clinical improvement + earlier discharge
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. RAPID FLUID REPLACEMENT increases CEREBRAL EDEMA risk in pediatric DKA. STANDARD = SLOW REPLACEMENT over 48 hours (NOT 24 hr like adult) to prevent osmotic shifts. Patient hemodynamics adequate after initial bolus + gradual replacement is safer + still effective.

opts.text: Use ORAL hydration (water + juice + clear liquids) for fluid replacement instead of IV; child can drink + this avoids IV access + reduces hospital cost
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. SEVERE DKA with altered mental status + severe dehydration + hyperosmolality + tachycardia + hypotension + severe acidosis = REQUIRES IV fluid + insulin therapy. Oral hydration alone INSUFFICIENT + can lead to aspiration in altered mental status + inadequate correction. Standard: IV fluid + insulin in PICU + later transition to oral as tolerating + stable. Patient cannot reliably take oral fluid in current state.

opts.text: WAIT to start insulin therapy until pH normalizes with fluid alone — insulin should not be given until acidosis improves to prevent rapid drop in pH + ketoacid clearance
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. INSULIN IS ESSENTIAL for ketoacid clearance + glucose lowering + acidosis resolution in DKA — without insulin, ketosis continues + acidosis worsens. Standard: START INSULIN INFUSION promptly after initial fluid bolus (typically 1 hr after fluid start) + continue throughout treatment. DELAY in insulin = prolonged DKA + worsening metabolic state.

steps.title: Take Action
